# Lesson-24863026

Water at 400 psi and 700f is in what thermodynamic state. So this question is probably simpler than what you might actually be asked on the PE exam. It's really a foundational question. You'll probably find yourself answering questions like this over and over again while solving more involved problems. And the question is, could you provide examples for water in other states, liquid saturated mixture and saturated vapor? So I actually challenged the individual who asked this question to come up with some answers on their own. And now I'll take a couple of minutes to talk about how you would do that. We can look in the steam tables and show that. So first of all, just answering the question, how do you know that water at 400 psi and 700f? What state that's in? So we can look in the saturated steam table and you can use either the saturated steam or the superheated steam either way will be able to get there. And in this case, the pressure was 400 and the temperature was 700. So we'll go down. This is two tables as you probably know the one organized by temperature and one organized by pressure. So if you go to the one organized by pressure drop down to 400, we'll notice that the corresponding saturation temperature for 400 psi is 444 degrees Fahrenheit. And in our case, we have a temperature of 700, which is far greater. So what does that mean? It's at that pressure, but a much higher temperature. So that means it cannot possibly be saturated. It must be at a superheated condition. So to answer the question more generally, you know, how can you come up with examples of one things would be in other states. Anytime you look at the steam tables and you have a pair of values, you have a corresponding pressure and temperature. It's saying that it is possible. Not only possible, but that you will be at a saturated state if you have this pressure and this temperature exactly. That doesn't necessarily mean that you have steam or liquid or some mixture. We don't actually know where you'll be along the line. Let me draw that on a saturation curve. So let's say you have the saturation curve here. This could be T s. We're saying you could be anywhere along that line from a saturated liquid to a saturated vapor or a mixture. So if you are along that line, you have the same temperature and pressure. And for example, that might be something like 400 psi and 700 degrees. What tells you how far you are along that line is either the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. You have to have another piece of information to tell you where you're being. So if the temperature is higher at that pressure, you know you're going to be out in the superheated region to the right of the saturation curve. Also, if you're at that temperature and the pressure is lower that would be another way of knowing that you're going to be in the superheated region. On the left side, you'll be down here in the sub cooled region. If the temperature is lower at that pressure. Or if the pressure is higher at that temperature. That's why saturated water is sometimes referred to as compressed water. So to come up with examples for ways to be in that other state. You could be a saturated mixture. And if you are at, for example, 400 psi and 444.6 degrees Fahrenheit. You could be a saturated mixture in such a case. And you could be a saturated vapor, saturated liquid or anywhere in between at that temperature and pressure. Again, you wouldn't know whether you were mixture unless you knew something else like the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. And the way that you would know that you're saturated liquid is if you had a higher pressure or a lower temperature. Then some pair from the saturated steam table. And you would know you're a superheated vapor. If you have a higher temperature or a lower pressure. Then the pair of values offered in the steam table. And you can do that for any pair of values. So yes, you're meant to multiply by the density to get the mass. And then you can do the mixing. So mass equals density times volume. We'll do that for both substances, water, and glycol. And then you can do a mixing calculation with those two masses. And just to sort of rationalize the answer, we know that for glycol, the density is higher, which means that the amount of mass per unit volume is going to be higher. This is the density of that tire. So the mass per unit volume is higher. And the specific heat capacity of glycol is lower. So you have more mass per unit volume. And you have a lower specific heat capacity. So not only is glycol going to drag down the blended specific heat capacity of the mixture, it's also going to do that in greater proportion than it's respective volume, because it has more mass per unit volume. So that's why whenever you're using glycol in a water mixing application, usually to avoid freezing, because it's going to go outside, you just want to use the minimum amount of glycol necessary and not more than that, because then you're not dragging down the thermal capacity of the fluid itself any more than you need to, just to avoid freezing.

All right, first one, thermo eight. Do we have to use the mass because the density of the two fluids is different? I originally solved it, just using the specific heat and volume. And this was the one if you recall about mixing water with glycol and figuring out what the specific heat capacity of that mixture would be. And the key to this problem was that I had to be done on a mass basis. It depends on how much mass of water and how much mass of glycol. And the trap is to do the mixing based on the volume percentages which leads to the wrong answer, which is the sensor here. And the way around that is to do exactly what the question is asking. So yes, you're meant to multiply by the density to get the mass. And then you can do the mixing. So mass equals density times volume. We'll do that for both substances, water, and glycol. And then you can do a mixing calculation with those two masses. And just to sort of rationalize the answer, we know that for glycol, the density is higher, which means that the amount of mass per unit volume is going to be higher. This is the density of that tire. So the mass per unit volume is higher. And the specific heat capacity of glycol is lower. So you have more mass per unit volume. And you have a lower specific heat capacity. So not only is glycol going to drag down the blended specific heat capacity of the mixture, it's also going to do that in greater proportion than it's respective volume, because it has more mass per unit volume. So that's why whenever you're using glycol in a water mixing application, usually to avoid freezing, because it's going to go outside, you just want to use the minimum amount of glycol necessary and not more than that, because then you're not dragging down the thermal capacity of the fluid itself any more than you need to, just to avoid freezing.

Water at 400 psi and 700f is in what thermodynamic state. So this question is probably simpler than what you might actually be asked on the PE exam. It's really a foundational question. You'll probably find yourself answering questions like this over and over again while solving more involved problems. And the question is, could you provide examples for water in other states, liquid saturated mixture and saturated vapor? So I actually challenged the individual who asked this question to come up with some answers on their own. And now I'll take a couple of minutes to talk about how you would do that. We can look in the steam tables and show that. So first of all, just answering the question, how do you know that water at 400 psi and 700f? What state that's in? So we can look in the saturated steam table and you can use either the saturated steam or the superheated steam either way will be able to get there. And in this case, the pressure was 400 and the temperature was 700. So we'll go down. This is two tables as you probably know the one organized by temperature and one organized by pressure. So if you go to the one organized by pressure drop down to 400, we'll notice that the corresponding saturation temperature for 400 psi is 444 degrees Fahrenheit. And in our case, we have a temperature of 700, which is far greater. So what does that mean? It's at that pressure, but a much higher temperature. So that means it cannot possibly be saturated. It must be at a superheated condition. So to answer the question more generally, you know, how can you come up with examples of one things would be in other states. Anytime you look at the steam tables and you have a pair of values, you have a corresponding pressure and temperature. It's saying that it is possible. Not only possible, but that you will be at a saturated state if you have this pressure and this temperature exactly. That doesn't necessarily mean that you have steam or liquid or some mixture. We don't actually know where you'll be along the line. Let me draw that on a saturation curve. So let's say you have the saturation curve here. This could be T s. We're saying you could be anywhere along that line from a saturated liquid to a saturated vapor or a mixture. So if you are along that line, you have the same temperature and pressure. And for example, that might be something like 400 psi and 700 degrees. What tells you how far you are along that line is either the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. You have to have another piece of information to tell you where you're being. So if the temperature is higher at that pressure, you know you're going to be out in the superheated region to the right of the saturation curve. Also, if you're at that temperature and the pressure is lower that would be another way of knowing that you're going to be in the superheated region. On the left side, you'll be down here in the sub cooled region. If the temperature is lower at that pressure. Or if the pressure is higher at that temperature. That's why saturated water is sometimes referred to as compressed water. So to come up with examples for ways to be in that other state. You could be a saturated mixture. And if you are at, for example, 400 psi and 444.6 degrees Fahrenheit. You could be a saturated mixture in such a case. And you could be a saturated vapor, saturated liquid or anywhere in between at that temperature and pressure. Again, you wouldn't know whether you were mixture unless you knew something else like the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. And the way that you would know that you're saturated liquid is if you had a higher pressure or a lower temperature. Then some pair from the saturated steam table. And you would know you're a superheated vapor. If you have a higher temperature or a lower pressure. Then the pair of values offered in the steam table. And you can do that for any pair of values. So that's a really fundamental skill. You'll get a lot of practice doing that while you're working thermoproblems and a whole variety of other things. I definitely have a video in the fundamentals about this, but I also redraw this picture several times as we're solving different problems. So if you haven't seen this before, you'll probably see it a couple times as you solve similar problems about pumps or fans. Anything driven by a motor. So what's happening when we have a motor is that we have electrical energy entering that motor. You can think of that in units of KW. If you like electrical power and you can give that different variable. Sometimes I use P with a subscript of E for electrical. More often than not these days, I'm seeing W dot work pre unit time as being the variable for power. So that's really what enters a motor and drives an AC motor to spin a shaft. And that spinning shaft is producing break horse power. So now we'll introduce the variable BHP. And when we talk about BHP, it's not only the variable, it's also the unit. So we'll use the same units as the variable named BHP. And then that's driving a pump in this case. And the output of the pump is the amount of power that actually goes into the fluid. In this case, it's water. So we call it water horse power. But if we wanted to be more general, we could call it hydraulic horse power or just fluid power would be fine as well. So water horse power WHP, again the name and the variable are the same. And now to get to your question, what's the difference between these two formulas while they're very similar? So WHP equals Q delta H over 30960. Start with that one. WHP equals Q delta H over 3960. Where Q is the volume flow rate in GPM. And delta H is the head and feet has to be in feet over 3960. Contrast that with the formula that you mentioned for BHP, which is GPM delta H times HG over 3960 times efficiency. So GPM and Q are the same thing. So I'll just write Q here, Q delta H still applies. And it's still going to be over 3960. But they're including an additional term in the numerator that is the specific gravity. And in the denominator, they're including the efficiency. Now, if we're talking about water, what is the specific gravity of water? It's just one, right? So water horse power has to be water by definition. So the brake horse power of a pump that's pumping water is going to have a specific gravity of one and this term goes away. So it's starting to look even more like this one. The only difference now is the inclusion of the efficiency. And that makes sense because there's a loss of efficiency at each stage in the process. As we go through the motor and turn those kilowatts into brake horse power, we lose a little something. How much do we lose? Well, it depends on the efficiency of the motor. And when we go from the power of a spinning shaft, the brake horse power to the WHP, the water horse power, we lose a little more energy. How much? While it depends on the efficiency of the pump. So this efficiency is the efficiency of the pump. That's implied as we're going from brake horse power. So basically, all we're saying, if this is an expression for the water horse power, plug that in here and the only additional pieces, the efficiency of the BHP, equals WHP divided by the efficiency of the pump. Or to say differently, we can get the efficiency of the pump by itself, multiply that over to the left and divide by the BHP. And we would say the efficiency of the pump then is the water horse power divided by the brake horse power. And I'm hoping that that makes very good intuitive sense because what is an efficiency, other than the percentage of what you could get, the all of the power of that spinning shaft that's driving the pump. But the numerator is less than that. It's what we actually are able to put into the water, which is the efficiency of the pump. So this is a key idea that you're not going to forget. And now we're able to sort of connect these two equations in the right way. And again, for water, specific gravity is one. So we're not going to worry about that. And delta H has to be in feet. So there's other versions of these. You'll see me use other flavors of these equations with slight differences in different cases. But I won't go through all of those here. I'd rather see you kind of discover those and apply those in the context of solving actual problems. I think it'll be more meaningful and more memorable to you if you just kind of encounter them naturally. But the friction factor is a function of the viscosity and the velocity and the roughness comes into it too. So think about that process that you go through when you use the Darcy equation, it's got the friction factor F and F is a function of the Reynolds number, which is how laminar or turbulent it is, right, where in what flow regime you're in. And then the relative roughness, which is epsilon over D. So if those things vary, then that's going to impact the friction factor and you'll get a different result. So in those cases, that's going to be your first choice and I think it should be, but I don't think you should know one to the exclusion of the other. I think you should know both. As for the next part of your question, can you eliminate minor losses? So I actually challenged the individual who asked this question to come up with some answers on their own. And now I'll take a couple of minutes to talk about how you would do that. We can look in the steam tables and show that. So first of all, just answering the question, how do you know that water at 400 psi and 700f? What state that's in? So we can look in the saturated steam table and you can use either the saturated steam or the superheated steam either way will be able to get there. And in this case, the pressure was 400 and the temperature was 700. So we'll go down. This is two tables as you probably know the one organized by temperature and one organized by pressure. So if you go to the one organized by pressure drop down to 400, we'll notice that the corresponding saturation temperature for 400 psi is 444 degrees Fahrenheit. And in our case, we have a temperature of 700, which is far greater. So what does that mean? It's at that pressure, but a much higher temperature. So that means it cannot possibly be saturated. It must be at a superheated condition. So to answer the question more generally, you know, how can you come up with examples of one things would be in other states. Anytime you look at the steam tables and you have a pair of values, you have a corresponding pressure and temperature. It's saying that it is possible. Not only possible, but that you will be at a saturated state if you have this pressure and this temperature exactly. That doesn't necessarily mean that you have steam or liquid or some mixture. We don't actually know where you'll be along the line. Let me draw that on a saturation curve. So let's say you have the saturation curve here. This could be T s. We're saying you could be anywhere along that line from a saturated liquid to a saturated vapor or a mixture. So if you are along that line, you have the same temperature and pressure. And for example, that might be something like 400 psi and 700 degrees. What tells you how far you are along that line is either the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. You have to have another piece of information to tell you where you're being. So if the temperature is higher at that pressure, you know you're going to be out in the superheated region to the right of the saturation curve. Also, if you're at that temperature and the pressure is lower that would be another way of knowing that you're going to be in the superheated region. On the left side, you'll be down here in the sub cooled region. If the temperature is lower at that pressure. Or if the pressure is higher at that temperature. That's why saturated water is sometimes referred to as compressed water. So to come up with examples for ways to be in that other state. You could be a saturated mixture. And if you are at, for example, 400 psi and 444.6 degrees Fahrenheit. You could be a saturated mixture in such a case. And you could be a saturated vapor, saturated liquid or anywhere in between at that temperature and pressure. Again, you wouldn't know whether you were mixture unless you knew something else like the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. And the way that you would know that you're saturated liquid is if you had a higher pressure or a lower temperature. Then some pair from the saturated steam table. And you would know you're a superheated vapor. If you have a higher temperature or a lower pressure. Then the pair of values offered in the steam table. And you can do that for any pair of values. So that's a really fundamental skill. You'll get a lot of practice doing that while you're working thermoproblems and a whole variety of other things. fluids nine instead of using the Darcy equation to find the head loss throughout the pipe. Is it acceptable to assume nominal roughness and take the head loss per hundred feet value listed in schedule 40 steel table for a three inch pipe? Then taking that number and multiplying it by the equivalent length. The answer is yes, it's a common question. My recommendation is to know how to do it both ways. I think using the Darcy equation is a bit more theoretical and maybe takes a little bit longer, but you should definitely know how to do it. Using the steel pipe friction tables is faster. It's equally reliable. So if you have a steel pipe, then there's no reason not to use the table. I usually go to the table as a first shot. But like I said, I think you should know both ways. Probably the next question that comes up after this question, so I'll address it here again. It's been addressed many times on office hours, but just because it's so frequent is the variance between the two methods. So if you use the Darcy equation, you may get one value and then use the table and you get another. And sometimes the relative error between those two approaches can be uncomfortably significant. It might be 50% or even double. I find that the steel pipe friction tables tend to be a little bit more conservative. But the key is to not look at the relative error, but to look at the absolute error. So even if you have 50 or 100% error, we might be talking about you know, two feet of loss compared to three or four feet of loss, but it sounds like a lot. But if you have a 300 feet of pipe, then on an absolute scale, it's pretty meaningless. So and that's not always the case. So I don't want to speak in absolute here. I'm saying all these things to sort of give you enough color to make some judgments on the day. And I don't think you should do every problem twice. I think in a lot of cases the answer choices won't be too close together. You're probably going to use the steel pipe friction tables and move on. But if you're given a friction factor or if something else about the problem, like it's not a steel pipe, let's say, then you know you're not going to be using the steel pipe friction table. So you're going to have Darcy as a fallback. So hopefully that addresses it and just to kind of finish the last part. Yes, taking that number and multiplying by the equivalent length. So yes, that's a perfectly valid method.

# Lesson-24863026

Water at 400 psi and 700f is in what thermodynamic state. So this question is probably simpler than what you might actually be asked on the PE exam. It's really a foundational question. You'll probably find yourself answering questions like this over and over again while solving more involved problems. And the question is, could you provide examples for water in other states, liquid saturated mixture and saturated vapor? So I actually challenged the individual who asked this question to come up with some answers on their own. And now I'll take a couple of minutes to talk about how you would do that. We can look in the steam tables and show that. So first of all, just answering the question, how do you know that water at 400 psi and 700f? What state that's in? So we can look in the saturated steam table and you can use either the saturated steam or the superheated steam either way will be able to get there. And in this case, the pressure was 400 and the temperature was 700. So we'll go down. This is two tables as you probably know the one organized by temperature and one organized by pressure. So if you go to the one organized by pressure drop down to 400, we'll notice that the corresponding saturation temperature for 400 psi is 444 degrees Fahrenheit. And in our case, we have a temperature of 700, which is far greater. So what does that mean? It's at that pressure, but a much higher temperature. So that means it cannot possibly be saturated. It must be at a superheated condition. So to answer the question more generally, you know, how can you come up with examples of one things would be in other states. Anytime you look at the steam tables and you have a pair of values, you have a corresponding pressure and temperature. It's saying that it is possible. Not only possible, but that you will be at a saturated state if you have this pressure and this temperature exactly. That doesn't necessarily mean that you have steam or liquid or some mixture. We don't actually know where you'll be along the line. Let me draw that on a saturation curve. So let's say you have the saturation curve here. This could be T s. We're saying you could be anywhere along that line from a saturated liquid to a saturated vapor or a mixture. So if you are along that line, you have the same temperature and pressure. And for example, that might be something like 400 psi and 700 degrees. What tells you how far you are along that line is either the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. You have to have another piece of information to tell you where you're being. So if the temperature is higher at that pressure, you know you're going to be out in the superheated region to the right of the saturation curve. Also, if you're at that temperature and the pressure is lower that would be another way of knowing that you're going to be in the superheated region. On the left side, you'll be down here in the sub cooled region. If the temperature is lower at that pressure. Or if the pressure is higher at that temperature. That's why saturated water is sometimes referred to as compressed water. So to come up with examples for ways to be in that other state. You could be a saturated mixture. And if you are at, for example, 400 psi and 444.6 degrees Fahrenheit. You could be a saturated mixture in such a case. And you could be a saturated vapor, saturated liquid or anywhere in between at that temperature and pressure. Again, you wouldn't know whether you were mixture unless you knew something else like the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. And the way that you would know that you're saturated liquid is if you had a higher pressure or a lower temperature. Then some pair from the saturated steam table. And you would know you're a superheated vapor. If you have a higher temperature or a lower pressure. Then the pair of values offered in the steam table. And you can do that for any pair of values. So that's a really fundamental skill. You'll get a lot of practice doing that while you're working thermoproblems and a whole variety of other things. So if you're early in the process, then it definitely makes sense to get a good hand along that sort of question isn't going anywhere. So I'm glad it came up and hopefully that clarifies.

# Lesson-24863026

Water at 400 psi and 700f is in what thermodynamic state. So this question is probably simpler than what you might actually be asked on the PE exam. It's really a foundational question. You'll probably find yourself answering questions like this over and over again while solving more involved problems. And the question is, could you provide examples for water in other states, liquid saturated mixture and saturated vapor? So I actually challenged the individual who asked this question to come up with some answers on their own. And now I'll take a couple of minutes to talk about how you would do that. We can look in the steam tables and show that. So first of all, just answering the question, how do you know that water at 400 psi and 700f? What state that's in? So we can look in the saturated steam table and you can use either the saturated steam or the superheated steam either way will be able to get there. And in this case, the pressure was 400 and the temperature was 700. So we'll go down. This is two tables as you probably know the one organized by temperature and one organized by pressure. So if you go to the one organized by pressure drop down to 400, we'll notice that the corresponding saturation temperature for 400 psi is 444 degrees Fahrenheit. And in our case, we have a temperature of 700, which is far greater. So what does that mean? It's at that pressure, but a much higher temperature. So that means it cannot possibly be saturated. It must be at a superheated condition. So to answer the question more generally, you know, how can you come up with examples of one things would be in other states. Anytime you look at the steam tables and you have a pair of values, you have a corresponding pressure and temperature. It's saying that it is possible. Not only possible, but that you will be at a saturated state if you have this pressure and this temperature exactly. That doesn't necessarily mean that you have steam or liquid or some mixture. We don't actually know where you'll be along the line. Let me draw that on a saturation curve. So let's say you have the saturation curve here. This could be T s. We're saying you could be anywhere along that line from a saturated liquid to a saturated vapor or a mixture. So if you are along that line, you have the same temperature and pressure. And for example, that might be something like 400 psi and 700 degrees. What tells you how far you are along that line is either the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. You have to have another piece of information to tell you where you're being. So if the temperature is higher at that pressure, you know you're going to be out in the superheated region to the right of the saturation curve. Also, if you're at that temperature and the pressure is lower that would be another way of knowing that you're going to be in the superheated region. On the left side, you'll be down here in the sub cooled region. If the temperature is lower at that pressure. Or if the pressure is higher at that temperature. That's why saturated water is sometimes referred to as compressed water. So to come up with examples for ways to be in that other state. You could be a saturated mixture. And if you are at, for example, 400 psi and 444.6 degrees Fahrenheit. You could be a saturated mixture in such a case. And you could be a saturated vapor, saturated liquid or anywhere in between at that temperature and pressure. Again, you wouldn't know whether you were mixture unless you knew something else like the quality or the enthalpy or the entropy. And the way that you would know that you're saturated liquid is if you had a higher pressure or a lower temperature. Then some pair from the saturated steam table. And you would know you're a superheated vapor. If you have a higher temperature or a lower pressure. Then the pair of values offered in the steam table. And you can do that for any pair of values. So that's a really fundamental skill. You'll get a lot of practice doing that while you're working thermoproblems and a whole variety of other things. So if you're early in the process, then it definitely makes sense to get a good hand along that sort of question isn't going anywhere. So I'm glad it came up and hopefully that clarifies.

All right, first one, thermo eight. Do we have to use the mass because the density of the two fluids is different? I originally solved it, just using the specific heat and volume. And this was the one if you recall about mixing water with glycol and figuring out what the specific heat capacity of that mixture would be. And the key to this problem was that I had to be done on a mass basis. It depends on how much mass of water and how much mass of glycol. And the trap is to do the mixing based on the volume percentages which leads to the wrong answer, which is the sensor here. And the way around that is to do exactly what the question is asking. So yes, you're meant to multiply by the density to get the mass. And then you can do the mixing. So mass equals density times volume. We'll do that for both substances, water, and glycol. And then you can do a mixing calculation with those two masses. And just to sort of rationalize the answer, we know that for glycol, the density is higher, which means that the amount of mass per unit volume is going to be higher. This is the density of that tire. So the mass per unit volume is higher. And the specific heat capacity of glycol is lower. So you have more mass per unit volume. And you have a lower specific heat capacity. So not only is glycol going to drag down the blended specific heat capacity of the mixture, it's also going to do that in greater proportion than it's respective volume, because it has more mass per unit volume. So that's why whenever you're using glycol in a water mixing application, usually to avoid freezing, because it's going to go outside, you just want to use the minimum amount of glycol necessary and not more than that, because then you're not dragging down the thermal capacity of the fluid itself any more than you need to, just to avoid freezing.

So let's take the first part of that question about when you actually needed Darcy Weissbeck in the fluid section or at least in the beginning of it. You'll notice I used the Darcy equation pretty frequently to solve problems. Later on when we get to the HVAC and systems and component sections, I rarely use it. I tend to go right to the steel pipe friction tables. Why is that? So, technically you should be able to use either one and then should reconcile, although they don't always perfectly reconcile and we've talked about that in a number of office hours in the past. But that's not really your question, your question here is kind of just which one do I use. And when you have to use the Darcy equation, the steel pipe friction tables and this might sound obvious, it has to be a steel pipe. So if the pipe is made out of anything else, you can't use those. So that's the first thing. Also, if the roughness is different, then you can't necessarily use the steel pipe tables. Although there is a correction table along the top of the steel pipe friction tables, where they give you different values. So, technically if they give you the other value for C, then maybe you could still use the table and that would be okay. But the friction factor is a function of the viscosity and the velocity and the roughness comes into it too. So think about that process that you go through when you use the Darcy equation, it's got the friction factor F and F is a function of the Reynolds number, which is how laminar or turbulent it is, right, where in what flow regime you're in. And then the relative roughness, which is epsilon over D. So if those things vary, then that's going to impact the friction factor and you'll get a different result. So it's kind of useful to know about that. And that's why I think the Darcy equation, even though it's a little bit more theoretical, it's a little bit more trustworthy for special cases and specific situations. But if you have like a problem that's like every other problem you've ever seen and it's water flowing in a steel pipe, it's pretty obvious that that question is it's just inviting you to go and use the steel pipe friction tables and that's quicker and easier. So in those cases, that's going to be your first choice and I think it should be, but I don't think you should know one to the exclusion of the other. I think you should know both. And if you're not sure which one to use then and if you have time, then it's not crazy to do it two ways and just see where it shakes out. But if you have water in a steel pipe or any fluid in a steel pipe, you're absolutely fine to use the steel pipe friction tables. That'll work out okay for you. As for the next part of your question, can you eliminate minor losses? And not necessarily, I would say if you have the length of the piping that you're dealing with, if it's in equivalent length, let's say it's a thousand feet. And maybe it's not physically a thousand feet, maybe it's physically 800 feet, but there's some elbows and fittings along the way, but it's an equivalent length of a thousand feet. Then you don't really care the distinction between the physical length. You don't care about the details. You just want the total equivalent length. But not every problem is always going to say equivalent length. It's just going to say length. And if that's the case, they're not trying to get you to think about, you don't have enough information anyway. You don't even tell you about any elbows or how many or what kind of valves or fittings are along the way. So you're just going to take that length and run with it. But if a problem starts talking about, there's this many, 90 degree elbows and they have such and such a k factor, or there's this kind of valve and that kind of valve, they're trying to get you to think about minor losses. Now it could turn out to be negligible, but that's a determination that you need to make. So I would not just go ahead and assume that you can eliminate minor losses. It's kind of like the velocity term and a lot of fluids problems. A lot of times it becomes negligible. And if you do enough problems and you figure out when it's going to matter and when it's not, like for example, on a pump, if the pipe is getting much smaller, then it's going to speed up a lot and you could end up with a meaningful difference on the velocity term in the pressure equation and the Bernoulli equation. Right? So you kind of learn that that's something to look out for. But if the pipe size isn't changing, the velocity is going to be pretty much flat, the velocity turns going to cross out. You get very used to crossing that out. It's kind of the same with this. If you just ignore minor losses because you've seen some problems where it only moved needle by 10 or 15, no. That may or may not be an acceptable decision to make. So I would be prepared to deal with the minor losses, but I would also expect that it may not make a difference in the final answer. And also there's a judgment piece to that because there's a directionality. You're always going to be adding more pressure for the minor losses. So if you omit it from your calculation, you're getting a minimum possible, possible pressure loss. But then if you go and make an answer choice selection, you select something that's higher than that, not something that's lower. So it's not always rounding to the nearest answer sometimes it's selecting the higher answer. And then for heat transfer, yeah, it's true, especially in heat transfer, there's a lot of problems you have to make assumptions just to get out of the gate and things can be off by 15 or 20%. Pretty easily. And that's okay. Like that's expected. And the answer choices can't be super close together as a result of that. And you just have to get comfortable with that. In fluids, I don't think that's quite as true, but there's still going to be that in any engineering problem, there's always tolerances and different problems have different tolerances. So part of the process that you're going to as you study is honing your judgment as to when you need to be really precise and when you need to kind of be in the ballpark and you have to pick your spots. And it's just not something that you can figure out from the top down. It's something that you only figure out from the bottom up by working lots of problems, by digging into the details. And maybe occasionally you take some shots and you omit some things or maybe you spend a lot of time including a level of detail that turns out wasn't actually necessary in the problem and you don't realize that until you go and look at the solution and you're like, okay, I guess I needed to do more here or I didn't need to do so much there and you kind of calibrate over time.

# Lesson-26324511

All right, first one, thermo eight. Do we have to use the mass because the density of the two fluids is different? I originally solved it, just using the specific heat and volume. And this was the one if you recall about mixing water with glycol and figuring out what the specific heat capacity of that mixture would be. And the key to this problem was that I had to be done on a mass basis. It depends on how much mass of water and how much mass of glycol. And the trap is to do the mixing based on the volume percentages which leads to the wrong answer, which is the sensor here. And the way around that is to do exactly what the question is asking. So yes, you're meant to multiply by the density to get the mass. And then you can do the mixing. So mass equals density times volume. We'll do that for both substances, water, and glycol. And then you can do a mixing calculation with those two masses. And just to sort of rationalize the answer, we know that for glycol, the density is higher, which means that the amount of mass per unit volume is going to be higher. This is the density of that tire. So the mass per unit volume is higher. And the specific heat capacity of glycol is lower. So you have more mass per unit volume. And you have a lower specific heat capacity. So not only is glycol going to drag down the blended specific heat capacity of the mixture, it's also going to do that in greater proportion than it's respective volume, because it has more mass per unit volume. So that's why whenever you're using glycol in a water mixing application, usually to avoid freezing, because it's going to go outside, you just want to use the minimum amount of glycol necessary and not more than that, because then you're not dragging down the thermal capacity of the fluid itself any more than you need to, just to avoid freezing.

So first up fluids 22, this is a general question that applies to problems using the steel pipe friction tables. In most cases, values from the steel pipe friction tables for pipe size and pipe diameter are extremely close and solving this problem using three inches instead of 3.068 inches still rounds to the correct answer. When time is critical, is it really necessary to include the extra sig figs or are there scenarios, pipe sizes that can yield significant differences? So my guidance here is about an 8 inch cut off. So if the pipe is 8 inches or bigger, I'm not going into the tables to get the exact value. Like it doesn't matter if it's 8 inches or 8.0, whatever. If it's a big pipe, so I'll just go ahead and round. If it says nominal or standard weight or standard weight steel, I'm not going into the table for that. But if it's smaller, if it's on the smaller side, it can make a difference. Now, nothing necessarily makes a difference because the answer choices might be spaced out a bit, but it could. And it's especially troubling when you get down to some smaller pipe sizes that are very close. You can have inch and a quarter, inch and a half and two inches, right? All very close. Also something weird happens with just the size of the wall, the pipe changes the inside diameter. Some one dramatically, not an absolute sense, but in a relative sense, as a percentage of that diameter. So that can really affect the internal area. And my guidance then is to always sweat the details for small pipes. And 8 inches might be a bit conservative, maybe you could cut it off at six inches or something like that. But the smaller the pipe, the more nervous I am about it, the larger the pipe, the less. So you can kind of maybe pick your own threshold there. And I suppose if you don't want the thousands place, you could just stop at the hundreds and maybe this thing becomes 3.07 or 3.1. But how much time are you really saving there? And I guess the general concern that I want to address here, which is not the so much the spirit of your question, but something I'm reading into is this notion that we're going to be rushing, right? When time is critical. I kind of want to take a big step back from that.

fluids nine instead of using the Darcy equation to find the head loss throughout the pipe. Is it acceptable to assume nominal roughness and take the head loss per hundred feet value listed in schedule 40 steel table for a three inch pipe? Then taking that number and multiplying it by the equivalent length. The answer is yes, it's a common question. My recommendation is to know how to do it both ways. I think using the Darcy equation is a bit more theoretical and maybe takes a little bit longer, but you should definitely know how to do it. Using the steel pipe friction tables is faster. It's equally reliable. So if you have a steel pipe, then there's no reason not to use the table. I usually go to the table as a first shot. But like I said, I think you should know both ways. Probably the next question that comes up after this question, so I'll address it here again. It's been addressed many times on office hours, but just because it's so frequent is the variance between the two methods. So if you use the Darcy equation, you may get one value and then use the table and you get another. And sometimes the relative error between those two approaches can be uncomfortably significant. It might be 50% or even double. I find that the steel pipe friction tables tend to be a little bit more conservative. But the key is to not look at the relative error, but to look at the absolute error. So even if you have 50 or 100% error, we might be talking about you know, two feet of loss compared to three or four feet of loss, but it sounds like a lot. But if you have a 300 feet of pipe, then on an absolute scale, it's pretty meaningless. So and that's not always the case. So I don't want to speak in absolute here. I'm saying all these things to sort of give you enough color to make some judgments on the day. And I don't think you should do every problem twice. I think in a lot of cases the answer choices won't be too close together. You're probably going to use the steel pipe friction tables and move on. But if you're given a friction factor or if something else about the problem, like it's not a steel pipe, let's say, then you know you're not going to be using the steel pipe friction table. So you're going to have Darcy as a fallback. So hopefully that addresses it and just to kind of finish the last part. Yes, taking that number and multiplying by the equivalent length. So yes, that's a perfectly valid method.

# Lesson-31593173

First up, TFS heat transfer number 10. The Newtult equation given the question calls out for RESABD. Why do you use the standard Reynolds equation? Well, they do reconcile. And I think what you're referring to in this question is the version of the Reynolds number, given in that particular section of the transfer, it's 5.3.7. And that's the internal flow. And the Reynolds number equation given there, this is for internal flow for convection, they use this subscript D. And they say that it's density times U sub m times diameter over the dynamic viscosity new. And the question is why did I just go ahead and use the sort of normal Reynolds number equation, which is V D over new. But actually it turns out that these are the same, because they define U sub m as the mean velocity. This is the mean velocity, which is basically the same as velocity, right? And what is kinematic viscosity? And viscosity is a function of dynamic viscosity. And density. So if we have standard density, then there's a direct linear relationship between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. So we can make the substitution into the equation of having new kinematic viscosity, the denominator instead of real and new. So then these two become exactly the same. So those formulas reconcile.

So that's actual pressure. We wanted to distinguish that from the volume metric compression ratio, which has to use that adjective volume metric has to be there. Then we could say that V2 over V1. So this idea that PV equals NRT or MRT, depending on how we're looking at it. But in either case, if the amount of mass or the number of moles is in changing, then this is a constant. And the specific as constant is a constant. And if the temperature weren't changing, then T would be a constant. And then, well, let's step back. So we can always say that PV is proportional to T. And if T was being held constant, then there would be a relationship between pressure and volume that they'd be inverse to one another. So that's to say that there would be a direct relationship. Just holding the temperature constant and saying that this equals one. Right? So then PV is proportional to one. So then P is proportional to one over the volume. So that means that P2 over P1 would be equal to V1 over V2. However, that's all based on the assumption that the temperature is constant. So that does not mean that if we have the pressure issue using the P, P2 over P1, that we can derive the volume metric compression ratio, we cannot. # Lesson-26324511

All right, first one, thermo eight. Do we have to use the mass because the density of the two fluids is different? I originally solved it, just using the specific heat and volume. And this was the one if you recall about mixing water with glycol and figuring out what the specific heat capacity of that mixture would be. And the key to this problem was that I had to be done on a mass basis. It depends on how much mass of water and how much mass of glycol. And the trap is to do the mixing based on the volume percentages which leads to the wrong answer, which is the sensor here. And the way around that is to do exactly what the question is asking. So yes, you're meant to multiply by the density to get the mass. And then you can do the mixing. So mass equals density times volume. We'll do that for both substances, water, and glycol. And then you can do a mixing calculation with those two masses. And just to sort of rationalize the answer, we know that for glycol, the density is higher, which means that the amount of mass per unit volume is going to be higher. This is the density of that tire. So the mass per unit volume is higher. And the specific heat capacity of glycol is lower. So you have more mass per unit volume. And you have a lower specific heat capacity. So not only is glycol going to drag down the blended specific heat capacity of the mixture, it's also going to do that in greater proportion than it's respective volume, because it has more mass per unit volume. So that's why whenever you're using glycol in a water mixing application, usually to avoid freezing, because it's going to go outside, you just want to use the minimum amount of glycol necessary and not more than that, because then you're not dragging down the thermal capacity of the fluid itself any more than you need to, just to avoid freezing.

TFS hydrology fluids 19 after watching the videos for fluid solutions and then going back to work problems. I'm wondering when it's really necessary to sorry, sure. Okay, asking when it's really necessary to use Darcy Weissbeck versus the friction loss tables. In fact, since the tables are conservative, could you also eliminate minor losses in most problems? And he transferred you spoke a lot about there being not much benefit and being specific. Since there were so many assumptions and estimates built in, it seems the same could be said for many if not most fluids problems dealing with losses and pipes. So let's take the first part of that question about when you actually needed Darcy Weissbeck in the fluid section or at least in the beginning of it. You'll notice I used the Darcy equation pretty frequently to solve problems. Later on when we get to the HVAC and systems and component sections, I rarely use it. I tend to go right to the steel pipe friction tables. Why is that? So, technically you should be able to use either one and then should reconcile, although they don't always perfectly reconcile and we've talked about that in a number of office hours in the past. But that's not really your question, your question here is kind of just which one do I use. And when you have to use the Darcy equation, the steel pipe friction tables and this might sound obvious, it has to be a steel pipe. So if the pipe is made out of anything else, you can't use those. So that's the first thing. Also, if the roughness is different, then you can't necessarily use the steel pipe tables. Although there is a correction table along the top of the steel pipe friction tables, where they give you different values. So, technically if they give you the other value for C, then maybe you could still use the table and that would be okay. But the friction factor is a function of the viscosity and the velocity and the roughness comes into it too. So think about that process that you go through when you use the Darcy equation, it's got the friction factor F and F is a function of the Reynolds number, which is how laminar or turbulent it is, right, where in what flow regime you're in. And then the relative roughness, which is epsilon over D. So if those things vary, then that's going to impact the friction factor and you'll get a different result. So it's kind of useful to know about that. And that's why I think the Darcy equation, even though it's a little bit more theoretical, it's a little bit more trustworthy for special cases and specific situations. But if you have like a problem that's like every other problem you've ever seen and it's water flowing in a steel pipe, it's pretty obvious that that question is it's just inviting you to go and use the steel pipe friction tables and that's quicker and easier. So in those cases, that's going to be your first choice and I think it should be, but I don't think you should know one to the exclusion of the other. I think you should know both. And if you're not sure which one to use then and if you have time, then it's not crazy to do it two ways and just see where it shakes out. But if you have water in a steel pipe or any fluid in a steel pipe, you're absolutely fine to use the steel pipe friction tables. That'll work out okay for you. As for the next part of your question, can you eliminate minor losses? And not necessarily, I would say if you have the length of the piping that you're dealing with, if it's in equivalent length, let's say it's a thousand feet. And maybe it's not physically a thousand feet, maybe it's physically 800 feet, but there's some elbows and fittings along the way, but it's an equivalent length of a thousand feet. Then you don't really care the distinction between the physical length. You don't care about the details. You just want the total equivalent length. But not every problem is always going to say equivalent length. It's just going to say length. And if that's the case, they're not trying to get you to think about, you don't have enough information anyway. You don't even tell you about any elbows or how many or what kind of valves or fittings are along the way. So you're just going to take that length and run with it. But if a problem starts talking about, there's this many, 90 degree elbows and they have such and such a k factor, or there's this kind of valve and that kind of valve, they're trying to get you to think about minor losses. Now it could turn out to be negligible, but that's a determination that you need to make. So I would not just go ahead and assume that you can eliminate minor losses. It's kind of like the velocity term and a lot of fluids problems. A lot of times it becomes negligible. And if you do enough problems and you figure out when it's going to matter and when it's not, like for example, on a pump, if the pipe is getting much smaller, then it's going to speed up a lot and you could end up with a meaningful difference on the velocity term in the pressure equation and the Bernoulli equation. Right? So you kind of learn that that's something to look out for. But if the pipe size isn't changing, the velocity is going to be pretty much flat, the velocity turns going to cross out. You get very used to crossing that out. It's kind of the same with this. If you just ignore minor losses because you've seen some problems where it only moved needle by 10 or 15, no. That may or may not be an acceptable decision to make. So I would be prepared to deal with the minor losses, but I would also expect that it may not make a difference in the final answer. And also there's a judgment piece to that because there's a directionality. You're always going to be adding more pressure for the minor losses. So if you omit it from your calculation, you're getting a minimum possible, possible pressure loss. So it's not always rounding to the nearest answer sometimes it's selecting the higher answer. And then for heat transfer, yeah, it's true, especially in heat transfer, there's a lot of problems you have to make assumptions just to get out of the gate and things can be off by 15 or 20%. Pretty easily. And that's okay. Like that's expected. And the answer choices can't be super close together as a result of that. And you just have to get comfortable with that. So part of the process that you're going to as you study is honing your judgment as to when you need to be really precise and when you need to kind of be in the ballpark and you have to pick your spots. # Lesson-31593173

First up, TFS heat transfer number 10. The Newtult equation given the question calls out for RESABD. Why do you use the standard Reynolds equation? Well, they do reconcile. And I think what you're referring to in this question is the version of the Reynolds number, given in that particular section of the transfer, it's 5.3.7. And that's the internal flow. And the Reynolds number equation given there, this is for internal flow for convection, they use this subscript D. And they say that it's density times U sub m times diameter over the dynamic viscosity new. And the question is why did I just go ahead and use the sort of normal Reynolds number equation, which is V D over new. But actually it turns out that these are the same, because they define U sub m as the mean velocity. This is the mean velocity, which is basically the same as velocity, right? And what is kinematic viscosity? And viscosity is a function of dynamic viscosity. And density. So if we have standard density, then there's a direct linear relationship between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. So we can make the substitution into the equation of having new kinematic viscosity, the denominator instead of real and new. So then these two become exactly the same. So those formulas reconcile.

# Lesson-31593173

First up, TFS heat transfer number 10. The Newtult equation given the question calls out for RESABD. Why do you use the standard Reynolds equation? Well, they do reconcile. And I think what you're referring to in this question is the version of the Reynolds number, given in that particular section of the transfer, it's 5.3.7. And that's the internal flow. And the Reynolds number equation given there, this is for internal flow for convection, they use this subscript D. And they say that it's density times U sub m times diameter over the dynamic viscosity new. And the question is why did I just go ahead and use the sort of normal Reynolds number equation, which is V D over new. But actually it turns out that these are the same, because they define U sub m as the mean velocity. This is the mean velocity, which is basically the same as velocity, right? And what is kinematic viscosity? And viscosity is a function of dynamic viscosity. And density. So if we have standard density, then there's a direct linear relationship between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. So we can make the substitution into the equation of having new kinematic viscosity, the denominator instead of real and new. So then these two become exactly the same. So those formulas reconcile.

fluids nine instead of using the Darcy equation to find the head loss throughout the pipe. Is it acceptable to assume nominal roughness and take the head loss per hundred feet value listed in schedule 40 steel table for a three inch pipe? Then taking that number and multiplying it by the equivalent length. The answer is yes, it's a common question. My recommendation is to know how to do it both ways. I think using the Darcy equation is a bit more theoretical and maybe takes a little bit longer, but you should definitely know how to do it. Using the steel pipe friction tables is faster. It's equally reliable. So if you have a steel pipe, then there's no reason not to use the table. I usually go to the table as a first shot. But like I said, I think you should know both ways. Probably the next question that comes up after this question, so I'll address it here again. It's been addressed many times on office hours, but just because it's so frequent is the variance between the two methods. So if you use the Darcy equation, you may get one value and then use the table and you get another. And sometimes the relative error between those two approaches can be uncomfortably significant. It might be 50% or even double. I find that the steel pipe friction tables tend to be a little bit more conservative. But the key is to not look at the relative error, but to look at the absolute error. So even if you have 50 or 100% error, we might be talking about you know, two feet of loss compared to three or four feet of loss, but it sounds like a lot. But if you have a 300 feet of pipe, then on an absolute scale, it's pretty meaningless. So and that's not always the case. So I don't want to speak in absolute here. I'm saying all these things to sort of give you enough color to make some judgments on the day. And I don't think you should do every problem twice. I think in a lot of cases the answer choices won't be too close together. You're probably going to use the steel pipe friction tables and move on. But if you're given a friction factor or if something else about the problem, like it's not a steel pipe, let's say, then you know you're not going to be using the steel pipe friction table. So you're going to have Darcy as a fallback. So hopefully that addresses it and just to kind of finish the last part. Yes, taking that number and multiplying by the equivalent length. So yes, that's a perfectly valid method.

# Lesson-32619286

fluids nine instead of using the Darcy equation to find the head loss throughout the pipe. Is it acceptable to assume nominal roughness and take the head loss per hundred feet value listed in schedule 40 steel table for a three inch pipe? Then taking that number and multiplying it by the equivalent length. The answer is yes, it's a common question. My recommendation is to know how to do it both ways. I think using the Darcy equation is a bit more theoretical and maybe takes a little bit longer, but you should definitely know how to do it. Using the steel pipe friction tables is faster. It's equally reliable. So if you have a steel pipe, then there's no reason not to use the table. I usually go to the table as a first shot. But like I said, I think you should know both ways. Probably the next question that comes up after this question, so I'll address it here again. It's been addressed many times on office hours, but just because it's so frequent is the variance between the two methods. So if you use the Darcy equation, you may get one value and then use the table and you get another. And sometimes the relative error between those two approaches can be uncomfortably significant. It might be 50% or even double. I find that the steel pipe friction tables tend to be a little bit more conservative. But the key is to not look at the relative error, but to look at the absolute error. So even if you have 50 or 100% error, we might be talking about you know, two feet of loss compared to three or four feet of loss, but it sounds like a lot. But if you have a 300 feet of pipe, then on an absolute scale, it's pretty meaningless. So and that's not always the case. So I don't want to speak in absolute here. I'm saying all these things to sort of give you enough color to make some judgments on the day. And I don't think you should do every problem twice. I think in a lot of cases the answer choices won't be too close together. You're probably going to use the steel pipe friction tables and move on. But if you're given a friction factor or if something else about the problem, like it's not a steel pipe, let's say, then you know you're not going to be using the steel pipe friction table. So you're going to have Darcy as a fallback. So hopefully that addresses it and just to kind of finish the last part. Yes, taking that number and multiplying by the equivalent length. So yes, that's a perfectly valid method.

# Lesson-40804540

How do you determine which specific volume of air to use for humidification problems? So I don't think my advice is any different for this than it is for problems that are not about humidification, whether it's humidification, dehumidification, heating, cooling, my guidance is generally the same. You want to find the specific volume that aligns with the volume flow rate that you have that you know. So for example, if you're doing in this problem, this is about humidification, so you're trying to find a mass flow rate of water. I'm skipping some of the foundational equations, but jumping right to the part that you're going to be using this at. You're going to be saying that the mass flow rate of water equals the mass flow rate of air times the difference in the humidity ratio, which you'll have quantified by that point. So this will be known. This will be what you want to know. And then the mass flow rate of air, you're going to say, well, that's the volume flow rate divided by the specific volume and your question is, well, which specific volume? And my question to your question is, which volume flow rate? Where do you know the volume flow rate? So for this problem, it's a little bit unclear. This is an interesting one. It says during winter operation a 2000 CFM outside air handling unit. So what is a 2000 CFM outside air handling unit? Is that a unit that takes in 2000 CFM about outside air? Or is that a unit that puts out 2000 CFM of air after it takes whatever it needs from the outside? Right? Because the density is changing the specific volume is changing slightly. And it's not obvious where the 2000 CFM is known. If it's known at the inlet, then I would take the specific volume at that outside condition. So if I call this one, I call that two. Then if I know Q1, I'm using V1. On the other hand, if I believe that I know the output CFM, then I'm using V2. So if you know in the problem where the volume flow rate is being given, you should use the corresponding specific volume to that. If you don't know, because it's not obvious and it's not obvious in this problem, then I would suggest using the entering condition. That's my suggestion that's served me well. I feel like that is going to be right, 9 to 95% of the time. And even out of some of the remaining 5 or 10% that's left over, when it's wrong, you still might get away with it because the amount of air may not be tremendous. And I'll show you what I mean by that. So let's say you assume that Q1 was for Q1. So you use the specific volume that's corresponds to Q1, you used V1. And that's for the entering condition, which is my proposal when you're in doubt. When in doubt, use the entering position and how wrong could you be? Well, in this problem, you would have gotten, you should have gotten 11.85. But if we were wrong to take that, we would have been at state 2, which would have been something like 13 and a half. And if you do the math on that, that could be anywhere from 10 to 15% error. So when you look at your answer choices, I'm looking at these answer choices. And the only ones that are close are C and D. So if I'm going for A or B, I have nothing to worry about. If I'm going for C or D, now maybe I got a worry, right, because that's going to be right in that range, maybe between 10 to 15%. So the correct answer is C or D, I'm going to be a little bit worried. But at least now, you have a sense of the direction and some of the judgments that you need to make. And if you do need to make a judgment call, then just do your best. We work to eliminate as much as we can, but we have to tolerate that there will be some looming uncertainty, even after all that effort. We should expect that. And that's just part of the process is being able to tolerate that and still keep going and that was faith in the process.

The radiation is pretty cut and dry that just gets kind of plugged in shog from the radiation formula. So I won't go too into that. Then for this piece, for the external convection, I should write that this is convection. So there was a duct and we knew the surface temperature T S and we knew the bulk ambient temperature. So to find the film temperature, we took the average of those two. Over to to find that average. And once we had that film temperature, that's the temperature which we looked up a whole bunch of different parameters that are needed to get the Rayleigh number and then you need a couple of constants C and N and then that gets pumped into another equation which all follows with the natural convection. So that's the big story is that this internal was treated as a forced convection problem and this external convection was treated as a natural convection problem and this piece was radiation. So did I need to put all three of those in the one problem for you probably not? But if you get a forced convection problem, you got to know to come down this path, you get natural convection. There's been four versions of it already, so we're up to version 1.4. So you want to make sure you get the latest and greatest version of that. You can download that as long as you have your NCS account set up, which you would have to do to register for the exam anyway. You can log in there and you can download. What I will say about the reference handbook is it's not a great learning aids. You can expect to kind of like glance through it and you may see some things that are familiar, but you're not going to learn by reading the reference handbook. # Lesson-61696162

TFS hydrology fluids 19 after watching the videos for fluid solutions and then going back to work problems. I'm wondering when it's really necessary to sorry, sure. Okay, asking when it's really necessary to use Darcy Weissbeck versus the friction loss tables. In fact, since the tables are conservative, could you also eliminate minor losses in most problems? And he transferred you spoke a lot about there being not much benefit and being specific. Since there were so many assumptions and estimates built in, it seems the same could be said for many if not most fluids problems dealing with losses and pipes. So let's take the first part of that question about when you actually needed Darcy Weissbeck in the fluid section or at least in the beginning of it. You'll notice I used the Darcy equation pretty frequently to solve problems. Later on when we get to the HVAC and systems and component sections, I rarely use it. I tend to go right to the steel pipe friction tables. Why is that? So, technically you should be able to use either one and then should reconcile, although they don't always perfectly reconcile and we've talked about that in a number of office hours in the past. But that's not really your question, your question here is kind of just which one do I use. And when you have to use the Darcy equation, the steel pipe friction tables and this might sound obvious, it has to be a steel pipe. So if the pipe is made out of anything else, you can't use those. So that's the first thing. Also, if the roughness is different, then you can't necessarily use the steel pipe tables. Although there is a correction table along the top of the steel pipe friction tables, where they give you different values. So, technically if they give you the other value for C, then maybe you could still use the table and that would be okay. But the friction factor is a function of the viscosity and the velocity and the roughness comes into it too. So think about that process that you go through when you use the Darcy equation, it's got the friction factor F and F is a function of the Reynolds number, which is how laminar or turbulent it is, right, where in what flow regime you're in. And then the relative roughness, which is epsilon over D. So if those things vary, then that's going to impact the friction factor and you'll get a different result. So it's kind of useful to know about that. And that's why I think the Darcy equation, even though it's a little bit more theoretical, it's a little bit more trustworthy for special cases and specific situations. But if you have like a problem that's like every other problem you've ever seen and it's water flowing in a steel pipe, it's pretty obvious that that question is it's just inviting you to go and use the steel pipe friction tables and that's quicker and easier. So in those cases, that's going to be your first choice and I think it should be, but I don't think you should know one to the exclusion of the other. I think you should know both. And if you're not sure which one to use then and if you have time, then it's not crazy to do it two ways and just see where it shakes out. But if you have water in a steel pipe or any fluid in a steel pipe, you're absolutely fine to use the steel pipe friction tables. That'll work out okay for you. As for the next part of your question, can you eliminate minor losses? And not necessarily, I would say if you have the length of the piping that you're dealing with, if it's in equivalent length, let's say it's a thousand feet. And maybe it's not physically a thousand feet, maybe it's physically 800 feet, but there's some elbows and fittings along the way, but it's an equivalent length of a thousand feet. Then you don't really care the distinction between the physical length. You don't care about the details. You just want the total equivalent length. But not every problem is always going to say equivalent length. It's just going to say length. And if that's the case, they're not trying to get you to think about, you don't have enough information anyway. You don't even tell you about any elbows or how many or what kind of valves or fittings are along the way. So you're just going to take that length and run with it. But if a problem starts talking about, there's this many, 90 degree elbows and they have such and such a k factor, or there's this kind of valve and that kind of valve, they're trying to get you to think about minor losses. Now it could turn out to be negligible, but that's a determination that you need to make. So I would not just go ahead and assume that you can eliminate minor losses. It's kind of like the velocity term and a lot of fluids problems. A lot of times it becomes negligible. And if you do enough problems and you figure out when it's going to matter and when it's not, like for example, on a pump, if the pipe is getting much smaller, then it's going to speed up a lot and you could end up with a meaningful difference on the velocity term in the pressure equation and the Bernoulli equation. Right? So you kind of learn that that's something to look out for. But if the pipe size isn't changing, the velocity is going to be pretty much flat, the velocity turns going to cross out. You get very used to crossing that out. It's kind of the same with this. If you just ignore minor losses because you've seen some problems where it only moved needle by 10 or 15, no. That may or may not be an acceptable decision to make. So I would be prepared to deal with the minor losses, but I would also expect that it may not make a difference in the final answer. And also there's a judgment piece to that because there's a directionality. You're always going to be adding more pressure for the minor losses. So if you omit it from your calculation, you're getting a minimum possible, possible pressure loss. But then if you go and make an answer choice selection, you select something that's higher than that, not something that's lower. So it's not always rounding to the nearest answer sometimes it's selecting the higher answer. And then for heat transfer, yeah, it's true, especially in heat transfer, there's a lot of problems you have to make assumptions just to get out of the gate and things can be off by 15 or 20%. Pretty easily. And that's okay. Like that's expected. And the answer choices can't be super close together as a result of that. And you just have to get comfortable with that. In fluids, I don't think that's quite as true, but there's still going to be that in any engineering problem, there's always tolerances and different problems have different tolerances. So part of the process that you're going to as you study is honing your judgment as to when you need to be really precise and when you need to kind of be in the ballpark and you have to pick your spots. And it's just not something that you can figure out from the top down. It's something that you only figure out from the bottom up by working lots of problems, by digging into the details. And maybe occasionally you take some shots and you omit some things or maybe you spend a lot of time including a level of detail that turns out wasn't actually necessary in the problem and you don't realize that until you go and look at the solution and you're like, okay, I guess I needed to do more here or I didn't need to do so much there and you kind of calibrate over time.

So WHP equals Q delta H over 30960. Start with that one. WHP equals Q delta H over 3960. Where Q is the volume flow rate in GPM. And delta H is the head and feet has to be in feet over 3960. Contrast that with the formula that you mentioned for BHP, which is GPM delta H times HG over 3960 times efficiency. So GPM and Q are the same thing. So I'll just write Q here, Q delta H still applies. And it's still going to be over 3960. But they're including an additional term in the numerator that is the specific gravity. And in the denominator, they're including the efficiency. Now, if we're talking about water, what is the specific gravity of water? It's just one, right? So water horse power has to be water by definition. So the brake horse power of a pump that's pumping water is going to have a specific gravity of one and this term goes away. So it's starting to look even more like this one. The only difference now is the inclusion of the efficiency. And that makes sense because there's a loss of efficiency at each stage in the process. As we go through the motor and turn those kilowatts into brake horse power, we lose a little something. How much do we lose? Well, it depends on the efficiency of the motor. And when we go from the power of a spinning shaft, the brake horse power to the WHP, the water horse power, we lose a little more energy. How much? While it depends on the efficiency of the pump. So this efficiency is the efficiency of the pump. That's implied as we're going from brake horse power. So basically, all we're saying, if this is an expression for the water horse power, plug that in here and the only additional pieces, the efficiency of the BHP, equals WHP divided by the efficiency of the pump. Or to say differently, we can get the efficiency of the pump by itself, multiply that over to the left and divide by the BHP. And we would say the efficiency of the pump then is the water horse power divided by the brake horse power. And I'm hoping that that makes very good intuitive sense because what is an efficiency, other than the percentage of what you could get, the all of the power of that spinning shaft that's driving the pump. But the numerator is less than that. It's what we actually are able to put into the water, which is the efficiency of the pump. So this is a key idea that you're not going to forget.
