# Lesson-47456806

So first up fluids 22, this is a general question that applies to problems using the steel pipe friction tables. In most cases, values from the steel pipe friction tables for pipe size and pipe diameter are extremely close and solving this problem using three inches instead of 3.068 inches still rounds to the correct answer. When time is critical, is it really necessary to include the extra sig figs or are there scenarios, pipe sizes that can yield significant differences? So my guidance here is about an 8 inch cut off. So if the pipe is 8 inches or bigger, I'm not going into the tables to get the exact value. Like it doesn't matter if it's 8 inches or 8.0, whatever. If it's a big pipe, so I'll just go ahead and round. If it says nominal or standard weight or standard weight steel, I'm not going into the table for that. But if it's smaller, if it's on the smaller side, it can make a difference. Now, nothing necessarily makes a difference because the answer choices might be spaced out a bit, but it could. And it's especially troubling when you get down to some smaller pipe sizes that are very close. You can have inch and a quarter, inch and a half and two inches, right? All very close. Also something weird happens with just the size of the wall, the pipe changes the inside diameter. Some one dramatically, not an absolute sense, but in a relative sense, as a percentage of that diameter. So that can really affect the internal area. And my guidance then is to always sweat the details for small pipes. And 8 inches might be a bit conservative, maybe you could cut it off at six inches or something like that. But the smaller the pipe, the more nervous I am about it, the larger the pipe, the less. So you can kind of maybe pick your own threshold there. And I suppose if you don't want the thousands place, you could just stop at the hundreds and maybe this thing becomes 3.07 or 3.1. But how much time are you really saving there? And I guess the general concern that I want to address here, which is not the so much the spirit of your question, but something I'm reading into is this notion that we're going to be rushing, right? When time is critical. I kind of want to take a big step back from that.

And when they initially did this, the reading that they got was 4700 CFM, which I didn't really know what that number meant. They said it meant that my house was pretty leaky. So then they go around and start sealing the holes, which is mostly done in the attic. So I already had insulation. The attic, I had like nine inches. They increased that to 15 by adding more fiberglass bats. They wanted to blow this stuff in there, but it just seemed messy. So I had them do the fiberglass instead. But before they did that, they pulled back all the insulation and they found the seams where the sheet rock meets and they sealed that with foam. And then they also put at the top of the attic that looked like this insulated barrier with a zipper. So that basically, if you think about the stack effect, which we all kind of know about, right, if you have a basement and it's cold in the winter, then you're getting little bits of infiltration in through any crevice that they can find because it's a little bit of negative pressure. And then the hot air rises and that wants to find its way up out of the condition space and it's the attic and then once it's in the attic, it's tad now and it's not doing any good. So any air that is being lost, it needs to be replaced. And this is sort of the flow in the winter. And yeah, so they went nuts sealing up there. They actually did the blower test with the basement door closed. So I think they were just focused on hitting the numbers and not maybe not doing the absolute best job. They possibly could. So I called them out a little bit for that. And then they said, no, you know, it matters, but you know, you don't have to, you don't have to focus too much on the basement. And I was like, no, we're going to seal around the basement too. So anyway, long story short after they did all that. This was the initial volume. They retested and they got it down to 1400 CFM. So that's something like a 70% reduction. Now they're doing that at 50 Pascal's, normally the delta P between inside your house and outside your house is not 50 Pascal's. It's much smaller, not that that's a lot of pressure, but it's smaller still. So if you think about what we know about the relationship between pressure and volume. It has kind of a squared relationship. So if they do their testing somewhere up here, we're going to ride down the system curve because the house is basically system. And we come to some location there, but it's non-linear. So if you have a linear reduction of 70% we'd end up somewhere here. So it's even better than that, which means that there's at least a 70% reduction in the amount of air that's continuously being changed in my house. And I've only had a few days to kind of look at the impact of the heating system. Of course the weather changes from day to day. So you can't put too much stock in that, but we're going to save a lot on our heating pills. Yeah, it's really interesting kind of project and the next step will be to go through. You get in the paperwork and everything ready so that we can do the heat pumps. And I'm excited to dig into that too. I could share a little bit more about some control stuff that I'm working on at home, but I think we have a critical mass here that you guys probably want to get to some exam problems. So let's jump to that. Thanks for indulging me for a few minutes here. Anyone want to want to stick around at the end. I'll gladly tell you about what I'm trying to do. So the question comes in when we're ready to write the conservation of energy piece, which was critical to this one. I'm a conservation of energy piece. We have to say that the same is true for the enthalpy. But when we do that, we have to make sure we write big H1 plus H2 equals H3. And I probably didn't write this in my solution. I probably jumped right to the next thing, which is expanding on that idea and what does that come from. So remember the distinction we covered this in the fundamentals pretty early on is probably one of the first 10 lessons. It distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic properties. So intrinsic property is per unit mass. And extrinsic property is just a total amount. So the total enthalpy extrinsic is big H and the intrinsic specific enthalpy is little H. So what is the definition of little H? Little H is big H per unit mass. Or to rearrange that, we want to get big H by itself. It's mass times the specific enthalpy, little H. So if we make that substitution for each part, we can say M.1. And I'm going to take the liberty of going from mass to a mass flow rate. So I'm going to put the dot. I have something that's between 4 and 10 and where exactly it lands if they're equal volume, then maybe it's half volume between, but it's not going to be additive. So that's why we can't add things with intrinsic properties. But we certainly can add things with extrinsic properties. So even though I didn't do it in the solution, if you were to start from here and use that idea to get to there. Hopefully that makes sense. Alright, first one's from Thermo 2. Number 6.2. You show CP units of BTU per pound ranking. The reference time book table 1.2.7 properties of air, low pressure, has units of BTU per pound degree Fahrenheit. Am I looking at the right table? Yes, you are looking at the right table. It's okay regardless of which unit is used there. Whether it's ranking or Fahrenheit, because the size of the Fahrenheit degree is the same as the size. Of a ranking degree. So one degree Fahrenheit is the same as one ranking. So which one is used in the denominator? It doesn't matter. The units are the same size. Fahrenheit degrees is the same as the ranking degree. The difference is that they have different zero points. So for example, if we were to say, what is absolute zero? Because absolute zero is absolute zero, no matter what you're using, right? It's just a different number that we're using to set the zero point. So if we're talking about Fahrenheit degrees, we would have to say that absolute zero is minus 460. But if we're talking about ranking, then it's just zero. So that's the same value. And then it just moves incrementally up and lock step from there. So minus 459 and plus 1 minus 458 and plus 2. But they move linearly together because the size of Fahrenheit degree is the same as the size of a ranking degree. And the same is true for Celsius and Kelvin by the way. So if you had Celsius degrees, you would have to say that this is minus 273 Celsius. Or you could say that it's zero. So that's the same number as the difference between the two. So if you think about that from the perspective of what the specificity capacity is actually doing, it's telling you the amount of BTO per pound degree Fahrenheit or BTO. This is the same thing as BTO per pound degree or anken. And since that's used in a formula where it's Q equals mcp delta t, the temperature change is always a delta. So the delta t, as measured in Fahrenheit, is the same as the delta t as measured in rank. So that doesn't really matter where absolute zero is because we're always using a delta there. So feel free to just, you know, whatever the units were in that chart, you can interchange them in an exchange rank and for Fahrenheit. You can't do that same sort of thing in other situations where, where it's not a delta and the absolute temperature is actually a matter that comes up in chemistry that comes up in other parts of thermos. So keep your ears peeled and your eyes tuned for the exceptions to that, but in this one it's fine to interchange.

# Lesson-54735870

First one, energy and power systems number three. I fell into your TRAF LOL. I got answer C and it was so confident I was right, but it was wrong that I used the wrong formula or was it wrong to assume that T sub 4 was 327.8 from the saturated water table. So this was a closed feedwater heater and you can model it like a heat exchanger, which you did. I like your picture here and I like that you kind of put the given information at each state and numbered the states 1 through 4 and the numbering is arbitrary, but more important is the arrows, it's coming and going from one side and what's coming and going from the other. And all of your labeling here is good. So the problem came in. It wasn't so much what you assumed for T sub 4. 4 was water, but 3 was superheated steam. So the heating source on this side is steam entering and liquid water leaving. And you used for that side of the heat exchanger to quantify the amount of heat that's being contributed by that steam 500 gpm delta T and that's a big no-no. Because that's only for liquid water. That's for the sensible heating or sensible cooling of liquid water 500 gpm delta T. That's what that rule is for. So if you don't have liquid water, you have liquid water leaving but you have saturated steam. Our superheated steam rather entering. Even if you had a saturated mixture, you wouldn't be able to use that because that rule of thumb doesn't accommodate phase change. It only deals with sensible heating or cooling. So that's what went wrong there. It wasn't that you had the temperature wrong. It was that you were using 500 gpm delta T. So take away here is whenever you have a situation where you have phase change, you have to use the enthalpy. So you should model q34 as m.delta h. And if you go about it that way and go into the steam tables, look up the enthalpy, then you'll be able to find your way through. So it wasn't really a trap. Although we did have an answer choice there that led you to answer choice C. So I guess I did that but yeah definitely wasn't looking to be tricky there.

# Lesson-54735870

First one, energy and power systems number three. I fell into your TRAF LOL. I got answer C and it was so confident I was right, but it was wrong that I used the wrong formula or was it wrong to assume that T sub 4 was 327.8 from the saturated water table. So this was a closed feedwater heater and you can model it like a heat exchanger, which you did. I like your picture here and I like that you kind of put the given information at each state and numbered the states 1 through 4 and the numbering is arbitrary, but more important is the arrows, it's coming and going from one side and what's coming and going from the other. And all of your labeling here is good. So the problem came in. It wasn't so much what you assumed for T sub 4. 4 was water, but 3 was superheated steam. So the heating source on this side is steam entering and liquid water leaving. And you used for that side of the heat exchanger to quantify the amount of heat that's being contributed by that steam 500 gpm delta T and that's a big no-no. Because that's only for liquid water. That's for the sensible heating or sensible cooling of liquid water 500 gpm delta T. That's what that rule is for. So if you don't have liquid water, you have liquid water leaving but you have saturated steam. Our superheated steam rather entering. Even if you had a saturated mixture, you wouldn't be able to use that because that rule of thumb doesn't accommodate phase change. It only deals with sensible heating or cooling. So that's what went wrong there. It wasn't that you had the temperature wrong. It was that you were using 500 gpm delta T. So take away here is whenever you have a situation where you have phase change, you have to use the enthalpy. So you should model q34 as m.delta h. And if you go about it that way and go into the steam tables, look up the enthalpy, then you'll be able to find your way through. So it wasn't really a trap. Although we did have an answer choice there that led you to answer choice C. So I guess I did that but yeah definitely wasn't looking to be tricky there.

# Lesson-55103809

TFS hydraulic and fluid applications 51. My question is more focused on how the drag force equation is derived for US customary. Why is gamma equals rho G over G C? But drag force is only divided by G C. I think the answer is that in this case we don't actually want gamma, the specific weight. We actually do want density to make the units work. So I don't really have a derivation, so much as just an explanation of the units working out. And I think that's the key. So the way the formula is given in the reference handbook is the force of drag is some drag coefficient, which is just a constant times density, not specific weight. I don't have a reason why that is others in to say that it is times velocity squared times area over to G C. And then when we kind of check the units on all of this, let's write out what these units are going to be just the units. So we'll skip the coefficient because that's just a constant. Density is going to have units of pound mass per cubic foot velocity would have units of feet per second and that's being squared. So we can make that feet squared and second squared. Area is going to be feet squared and then denominator G C has units of pound mass foot per second squared over pound force. So let's take a look. We have four feet in the numerator with three in denominator and another one. So all the feet go away. Second squared are both in denominator so that goes away. Pound mass goes away. And the only thing left is pound force in the denominator of the denominator so that comes all the way up. So ultimately these units are just pound force, which is what we want for a force. So I know that's not a derivation, but it just kind of shows how if you plug in all the things with the units as they're probably given that we do in fact end up with pound force in exactly this arrangement. So we wouldn't want to go and insert another G in here that would just add units that we don't want or need. There may be something more fundamental that your question is driving at, which is, why is it that the force of drag is a function of the density only, whereas the buoyant force is a function of specific weight. And I think if that's what we're kind of veering toward then, then that's kind of more about how buoyancy is fundamentally different from drag, I guess, in terms of what physical phenomena are present that cause those forces to be generated. Hope that helps at least point in the right direction.

And if the isentropic assumption is stated in the problem statement, then you don't have to go that route, but it's definitely something you want to know how to do.

The first one's from TFS Thermal 4. How can we know if it's a closed system or an open system? The work equation is different in each. This one was about, I see, drop at compression of air. And the intent of the question was to assume that it was a closed system. That wasn't explicitly stated. There was no information though about any mass transfer or volume flow rate or anything like that. There wasn't any statement about it being reversible. An isentropic process that's for an open system would usually be called reversible. And the other thing that I'll mention is, you may be thinking, OK, well, but it's probably a compressor. So there's got to be some mass flow rate going in and out of this thing. But remember that you get to draw the system boundary around whatever components you like. So you could draw it around the compressor and the supporting piping distribution or tubing or whatever the case may be. So there's enough there. It's not about explicitly stating it. I could just state in the problem statement that it's a closed system. But it's about the absence of details that would probably be included if it was an open system that kind of tips the scales in that direction. But you're right. It is a little bit ambiguous. So probably better to just state it outright. But hopefully didn't three to far off track. And yeah. So closed system for that one.

# Lesson-61696162

TFS hydrology fluids 19 after watching the videos for fluid solutions and then going back to work problems. I'm wondering when it's really necessary to sorry, sure. Okay, asking when it's really necessary to use Darcy Weissbeck versus the friction loss tables. In fact, since the tables are conservative, could you also eliminate minor losses in most problems? And he transferred you spoke a lot about there being not much benefit and being specific. Since there were so many assumptions and estimates built in, it seems the same could be said for many if not most fluids problems dealing with losses and pipes. So let's take the first part of that question about when you actually needed Darcy Weissbeck in the fluid section or at least in the beginning of it. You'll notice I used the Darcy equation pretty frequently to solve problems. Later on when we get to the HVAC and systems and component sections, I rarely use it. I tend to go right to the steel pipe friction tables. Why is that? So, technically you should be able to use either one and then should reconcile, although they don't always perfectly reconcile and we've talked about that in a number of office hours in the past. But that's not really your question, your question here is kind of just which one do I use. And when you have to use the Darcy equation, the steel pipe friction tables and this might sound obvious, it has to be a steel pipe. So if the pipe is made out of anything else, you can't use those. So that's the first thing. Also, if the roughness is different, then you can't necessarily use the steel pipe tables. Although there is a correction table along the top of the steel pipe friction tables, where they give you different values. So, technically if they give you the other value for C, then maybe you could still use the table and that would be okay. But the friction factor is a function of the viscosity and the velocity and the roughness comes into it too. So think about that process that you go through when you use the Darcy equation, it's got the friction factor F and F is a function of the Reynolds number, which is how laminar or turbulent it is, right, where in what flow regime you're in. And then the relative roughness, which is epsilon over D. So if those things vary, then that's going to impact the friction factor and you'll get a different result. So it's kind of useful to know about that. And that's why I think the Darcy equation, even though it's a little bit more theoretical, it's a little bit more trustworthy for special cases and specific situations. But if you have like a problem that's like every other problem you've ever seen and it's water flowing in a steel pipe, it's pretty obvious that that question is it's just inviting you to go and use the steel pipe friction tables and that's quicker and easier. So in those cases, that's going to be your first choice and I think it should be, but I don't think you should know one to the exclusion of the other. I think you should know both. And if you're not sure which one to use then and if you have time, then it's not crazy to do it two ways and just see where it shakes out. But if you have water in a steel pipe or any fluid in a steel pipe, you're absolutely fine to use the steel pipe friction tables. That'll work out okay for you. As for the next part of your question, can you eliminate minor losses? And not necessarily, I would say if you have the length of the piping that you're dealing with, if it's in equivalent length, let's say it's a thousand feet. And maybe it's not physically a thousand feet, maybe it's physically 800 feet, but there's some elbows and fittings along the way, but it's an equivalent length of a thousand feet. Then you don't really care the distinction between the physical length. You don't care about the details. You just want the total equivalent length. But not every problem is always going to say equivalent length. It's just going to say length. And if that's the case, they're not trying to get you to think about, you don't have enough information anyway. You don't even tell you about any elbows or how many or what kind of valves or fittings are along the way. So you're just going to take that length and run with it. But if a problem starts talking about, there's this many, 90 degree elbows and they have such and such a k factor, or there's this kind of valve and that kind of valve, they're trying to get you to think about minor losses. Now it could turn out to be negligible, but that's a determination that you need to make. So I would not just go ahead and assume that you can eliminate minor losses. It's kind of like the velocity term and a lot of fluids problems. A lot of times it becomes negligible. And if you do enough problems and you figure out when it's going to matter and when it's not, like for example, on a pump, if the pipe is getting much smaller, then it's going to speed up a lot and you could end up with a meaningful difference on the velocity term in the pressure equation and the Bernoulli equation. Right? So you kind of learn that that's something to look out for. But if the pipe size isn't changing, the velocity is going to be pretty much flat, the velocity turns going to cross out. You get very used to crossing that out. It's kind of the same with this. If you just ignore minor losses because you've seen some problems where it only moved needle by 10 or 15, no. That may or may not be an acceptable decision to make. So I would be prepared to deal with the minor losses, but I would also expect that it may not make a difference in the final answer. And also there's a judgment piece to that because there's a directionality. You're always going to be adding more pressure for the minor losses. So if you omit it from your calculation, you're getting a minimum possible, possible pressure loss. But then if you go and make an answer choice selection, you select something that's higher than that, not something that's lower. So it's not always rounding to the nearest answer sometimes it's selecting the higher answer. And then for heat transfer, yeah, it's true, especially in heat transfer, there's a lot of problems you have to make assumptions just to get out of the gate and things can be off by 15 or 20%. Pretty easily. And that's okay. Like that's expected. And the answer choices can't be super close together as a result of that. And you just have to get comfortable with that. # Lesson-62413708

Starting off with TFS Energy Empower Systems 18. First, would it be appropriate to use the ideal gas relationship T2 over T1? It's P2 over P1, raised to the K-minus 1 over K. To find T2, and then redandle P from an air table, it gave a very similar answer to your approach using PR1 over PR2 equals P2 over P1. Let's just start with the first question. Yes, very reasonable to do that. I think no both ways, and if they're constant, that's a good reassuring sign. Second question, why is it okay to use properties of air at low pressure when the pressure is for atmosphere? What pressure would be considered to hide use? What would you do or need in that situation? Good question, this question comes up pretty often too. Technically, the air at low pressure table, there's not, nobody is going to give you a number of hits that were that cuts off. In my experience, the tends to remain valid up to surprisingly, what you might consider to be high pressure. I've never run into a problem using this table even when it's 10 times atmospheric pressure. Technically, this relationship between pressure and temperature. The real answer here is that you're going to get some deviation from ideal properties. Ideal properties are by definition at, I don't know what's second. Okay, everything in technically the low pressure air table is supposed to be one atmosphere. But like I said, you can use it up to pressures that are higher than that. And you'll usually get away with it. The further you go, the more deviation they'll be, but you might only get a few percent deviation even for orders of magnitude more pressure like 10 atmospheres instead of 1. So it's a pretty useful table and you want to keep it in play if you can. If the pressure gets so high, and I don't know, again, I don't have a number to give you as to what that caught off is, we can no longer consider the air's behavior to be ideal. We have to shift our thinking from ideal gas to real gas behavior. And real gas behavior, you may remember this from your chemistry days, is based on something called the compressibility factor. We always say PV equals MRT, that's the ideal gas law. And then we can divide both sides by mass so that this becomes specific volume. And then it becomes P times little V equals RT. So this is the same idea, these are both the ideal gas law. For real gases, we introduce something called the compressibility factor, which is, because they're variable Z. So it becomes P little V equals Z RT. And you can look up in the reference handbook, the compressibility factor or compressibility chart. And you'll see ways that you can find this value of Z, depending on something called the critical temperature and the critical pressure. And then you can use those two to calculate another parameter. And then you use this table to look up the Z value. Really doesn't come up very much on the P exam, which is why we don't spend a lot of time covering it. But that is the technical answer to your question. You know, what happens when the gas behavior can no longer be considered ideal? We go into real gas territory. And in real life applications, they would probably be databases for this. And you probably be using software. It's a pretty narrow set of problems where you'd actually be able to calculate a Z value of compressibility factor. So it becomes a bit contrived and it comes up on exams and chemistry tests, so that they can have you prove that you know this. But it's just not an often examined topic. So say all that, say you can use this air table a lot more than you probably think. And you know, unless the problem is really hinting you toward getting into real gases and compressibility factor, I wouldn't go that direction. But I do want to kind of give you that context.

So it becomes P little V equals Z RT. And you can look up in the reference handbook, the compressibility factor or compressibility chart. And you'll see ways that you can find this value of Z, depending on something called the critical temperature and the critical pressure. And then you can use those two to calculate another parameter. And then you use this table to look up the Z value. Really doesn't come up very much on the P exam, which is why we don't spend a lot of time covering it. But that is the technical answer to your question. You know, what happens when the gas behavior can no longer be considered ideal? We go into real gas territory. And in real life applications, they would probably be databases for this. And you probably be using software. It's a pretty narrow set of problems where you'd actually be able to calculate a Z value of compressibility factor. So it becomes a bit contrived and it comes up on exams and chemistry tests, so that they can have you prove that you know this. But it's just not an often examined topic. So say all that, say you can use this air table a lot more than you probably think. And you know, unless the problem is really hinting you toward getting into real gases and compressibility factor, I wouldn't go that direction. But I do want to kind of give you that context.

# Lesson-63254300

HVSC6, why does the infiltrated air in this problem get taken into consideration as a part of the total cooling delivered by the ventilation system? Isn't infiltrated air defined as air that enters the building via miscellaneous means, such as open doors, open windows, and building leakage? I would normally assume that this gets treated separately than air brought in via the building via a dedicated ventilation system. Yeah, you're absolutely right about the definition of infiltrated air. It's exactly what you described, open doors, open windows, and just general leakage through the building envelope. Or if there's anything that's not sealed. So there's a small amount of air exchange that happens, and that's infiltration, and that's separate and distinct from ventilation, which is air that's deliberately brought in to satisfy fresh air requirements. And this particular problem put them together. So there's this much ventilation required, and then there's this many air changes per hour of infiltration as well. And it wasn't meant to be a trick question. All I wanted to do was have you deal with both of those together and add them, and then have the cooling system, calculate the load. That is attributable to both the infiltrated air and the fresh outside air. And if you assume that, then it was pretty straightforward, but if you thought it was a trick question, and you went and omitted the ventilation air, and just dealt with, sorry, omitted the infiltration air, just dealt with the ventilation air. Then of course you would get a much smaller answer. So it's a fair question. Again, I wasn't intending to trick you. I guess the embedded assumption in this problem is that the outside air system is the same as the main cooling system. So it's dealing with the ventilation and the infiltration, and also probably the internal cooling load as well, which wasn't noted in the problem statement. So I guess that could be a little bit confusing, confusing, but yeah, I just wanted to give you the information you needed, have you use it, get the answer, and not trying to be tricky there. And to your point, the outside air system or the fresh air system, if you want to call that, is oftentimes separate and distinct from the main cooling system. If that's what you're accustomed to, that's what you're used to seeing and buildings that you work in or other practice problems that you've solved, that's not unusual. So I understand the confusion.

So if it's 5 psi g saturated steam that's entering, then it's immediately going through phase change and we don't know how far along that path it gets. It could end up as a saturated liquid. But usually most of the heat transfers the result of the late in heat transfer, which is consequence of the phase change, which is one of the reasons why we use a steam system is because that phase change has the ability to pack a big punch in terms of how much heat it can deliver. It's sort of the thing that makes refrigerant based systems and heat pumps work too. We're leveraging the phase change in particular to really get a lot of heat to transfer. If we just had sensible heating and cooling only, that would be worth something but it's not quite as potent as what we can do when there's phase change. So and then somewhere downstream of that, just if you're kind of zooming out and thinking about the bigger picture, there would eventually be some kind of pressure reducing mechanism before we get to the condensate return. The first one is from Thermo 8. On the solution you've used 19.47 psi to look up each one and h2 for the saturated steam. Should the steam side be treated as a closed loop system and stays at 5 psi g unless stated as open to the atmosphere at the Connancy tank or drain. So this problem was a steam heat exchanger. So on the primary side we have this steam coming in that we've been told is 5 psi g saturated steam and it's being used to heat liquid water on the second side. So the answer to the question should the steam be treated as closed loop and stay at 5 psi g as yes? So when we say we have this steam that's entering at a particular pressure, there's no pressure reducing mechanism in the coil. It's being served up at that pressure. And what's mainly happening in the process of heating is that that steam is undergoing phase change. So it's condensing and in so doing it's giving up its late in heat energy and that's being transmitted to the liquid water that we're heating. So if it's 5 psi g saturated steam that's entering, then it's immediately going through phase change and we don't know how far along that path it gets. It could end up as a saturated liquid. It could end up as a saturated mixture where there's still some steam in the system or it could end up, there could be some sub cooling where it goes all the way to being a saturated liquid and then continues to cool sensibly from there. But usually most of the heat transfers the result of the late in heat transfer, which is consequence of the phase change, which is one of the reasons why we use a steam system is because that phase change has the ability to pack a big punch in terms of how much heat it can deliver. It's sort of the thing that makes refrigerant based systems and heat pumps work too. We're leveraging the phase change in particular to really get a lot of heat to transfer. If we just had sensible heating and cooling only, that would be worth something but it's not quite as potent as what we can do when there's phase change. So and then somewhere downstream of that, just if you're kind of zooming out and thinking about the bigger picture, there would eventually be some kind of pressure reducing mechanism before we get to the condensate return. If you're thinking about like the steam distribution system as a whole, so yeah, eventually you're right, the pressure is reduced but that would not be assumed to be the case for the particular coil that we're analyzing. So just to kind of put a finer point on that before we move on, if you're thinking about the vapor dome, we have state one entering is the saturated steam condition and then state two leaving. If it's a saturated liquid, then it would be here state two. If it's a mixture, it might be somewhere like here and if there's some sub cooling, then it might be somewhere like like here.

And they might use the big A or the little A. I'm not sure exactly how it shakes out in the handbook, but fundamentally there are two different equations and they do call them one of them the existing conditions equation. And the other one is more of a theoretical version. So the theoretical version is used in a design application. If you haven't already built something, you don't know exactly how it's going to perform. So you have to make assumptions, not assumptions, but you know you use sort of the design criteria to figure out what the NPSHA will be. And then the other one is for something that's already built. That's why it's called the existing conditions, where you have gauges that can read the pressure directly. So this question has come up a number of times before, and you can look back at previous office hours archive to hear a little bit more about my answer to that. Now, nothing about the concept of net positive suction had available has changed in a long time. So it's not like the murm is different. It's just presented a little bit differently in the reference handbook. So when I did the update, I made an update to the written solution in the book. So if you take a look at the solution PDF, you'll find a version of that equation that aligns perfectly with the reference handbook. So I would encourage you to consult that as a first shot. And the volume is changing as a function of the temperature because it we're assuming that it's an ideal gas. So let's write the ideal gas so I think that'll help eliminate this a little bit more. So the ideal gas law tells us that P times V equals MRT. And in such a case where the pressure is being held constant, the mass isn't changing and R is the universal gas constant. Then the only things that can change are the volume and the temperature. So there's a proportionality between the manner in which the volume and the temperature are changing. So changes in volume are kind of a proxy for changes in temperature. So it's not unreasonable to expect to see a delta T in the formula that we're ultimately going to use to calculate work. So work is big W over M and then that would just be RT. I'll answer the second question first. That's a mistake on my part. This is a computational error there. And your number is right. My number is wrong. So I have to put a correction on that one. I haven't gotten around to it mostly because he is still going to pick answer choice B, but you have a sharp pencil there. But the more interesting question is the first one. So this is like a first law formulation when we have mass flow rates, times enthalpy, velocity, and elevation, and the same thing for the exit condition, for the compressor, and then Q and W out. So this is like more foundational fundamental for the HVAC folks if you do a lot of Bernoulli so they're jumping straight to that. But this is sort of like the fundamental equation that's behind all that. And it's the right way to deal with the compressor. So once we have this equation written down, the question is, how do we actually put it all together? And something interesting happens with the units. And you got to get into the details on this one. There's no way to kind of just figure it out at a glance looking at the problem. If you think about, you've got a mass flow rate. So you've got BT, you've got pounds per hour, or pounds per unit time in somewhere or another. And then for the enthalpy, we're going to have BT use per pound. So we're going to end up with an energy flow rate. We're going to end up with BT use per hour or BT use per some unit time. And that makes sense. But the problem is, if you look at that velocity term and the elevation term, they're not going to work out to be in units that can just be added to BT use per pound. So let's jot some of this down on a whiteboard. Those enthalpy terms are going to want to be BTU per pound. And that's really what we expect what we want. But that middle term, that kinetic energy term, that's going to have units of, let's take a look at it, V squared over two, two doesn't do anything. But the velocity is probably going to be something like feet per seconds, feet per second if you're in US customer. So that's going to turn out to be feet squared per second squared. And then that elevation term, if it is G times Z, where Z is going to be height in feet, and G is feet per second squared. So let's call that potential energy term. But it's gravitational potential energy. It's attributable to height elevation. So that's going to end up being feet squared over second squared as well. So if you just start cranking on numbers, you might get to thinking that these terms are significant. And they may or may not be. But before we add them, we need to get them into like units. And the way we do that is we use the idea that one BTU equals 778 foot pounds. And that's pound force. So how do we actually use that? Well, let's start with one of these two and imagine we wanted to get it into BTU per pound. So if we start with feet per second squared or feet squared per second squared, we're going to be multiplying that by one BTU over 778 foot pounds. And this is not going to do everything we want. It's going to do some of the things we want. It'll get rid of one of the feet right off the bat. And it'll introduce BTUs, which is a good thing. But we're going to need pound mass because this BTU per pound is the pound mass. So how do we get that into the denominator? Well, we need everybody's favorite hidden unit or hidden variable, which is the gravitational constant GC. But don't worry too much about GC. You don't stress about it. Just think about what you need. And the idea here is that, while one pound force equals 32.2 pound mass foot per second squared. So if we want to get rid of pound force and introduce pound mass, we know we're going to have this in the numerator. So let's write that up here. One pound force. And then our denominator is 32.2 pound mass foot per second squared. And rather than write a denominator, the denominator, I'm going to take the liberty of bringing the second squared up here. So what have we done now? We've gotten rid of pound force. We've gotten rid of second squared. And we've got another foot to cancel with this foot. So we're left with BTU per pound mass, which is what we want. And now we're going to achieve alignment with this. But we've divided by 738 and 32.2, which is like dividing by 25,051. Check me on that. So that's quite a bit smaller. And that's why these terms, if you're just racing to put numbers in, they might seem meaningful. But then when we divide them by this, all of a sudden, they turn into not much, especially the velocity term. You could have a situation where there's a significant elevation that you're dealing with. Although I can't imagine that with a compressor, in this case, we just assumed that compressors are as an adult and that inlet have the same vertical center line so that we struck out immediately. But maybe there could be some scenario where that comes into play. I feel like the potential energy is more likely to come into play. And with the velocity it rarely does. But it's there. It's part of the equation. So if you're not comfortable assuming that it doesn't matter, then include it and then strike it out when you realize it's too small. And then as you solve more and more problems, you may start to develop a judgment for this. And you may start to initially you're like, oh, I don't know if I'm going to strike this out or not, but let me see. And then later on, you're like, I'm pretty sure I'm going to let me double check. And then eventually you're like, I'm sure I'm not going to need this. But you don't want to start from a place of certainty. I think it's a good question to ask and you should be asking it every time you encounter this type of problem. And maybe they'll come a moment where you'll decide to cross something out and never look back. And that's when you know you don't need to lose any sleep over it. So just to put this in perspective, if you have something like a hundred feet squared per second squared, that's going to turn into 0.004 B2 per pound. And that was kind of the situation in this problem. And the Antelpies that we were dealing with in this problem were large. They were both more than a hundred B2 per pound. So that this is minuscule compared to that. Doesn't doesn't move the needle.
